item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The insistence in the text that I am a fish are interesting. Are we to interpret them as an attempt to convey the effect of the book, that the text isn't actually there it's just my mind being affected and seeing things that try to convince me that I am a fish but since I haven't seen the book itself they are not that strong? Or is the text actually there, is the book's anomalous power spreading into the material describing it and altering what the file literally says, suggesting some awareness on the part of the book/effect?

How you interpret that also bleeds into the next interesting ambiguity: Exactly how much control you think the Foundation has over it. Are they experts and well aware of how to contain it, and have just accepted that any text that mentions it will have these alterations or give this type of hallucination to the reader? Or are they baffled and overwhelmed, struggling against a power they don't understand and where even their attempts to document it fail and are made a conduit of it's effect by the object?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since every other Dr. mentioned related to the effect, I got curious about Dr. Albert Salernus.

Google came up with one "Magister Salernus", a medieval alchemist who invented fractional distillation of alcohol. He was also a founding member of the "School of Salernno". Which was the first medical school in medieval Europe.

Doesn't really connect to being a fish, but seems like he would at least qualify for an honorary doctorate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Imagine if the book did turn you into a fish. But the memetic properties were it took people a while to percieve you as one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to downvote. The gimmick did nothing for me, I did it better as a toaster. Besides, the ex-library doesn't make -you- a library. It just was a library, 'cause it used to be a library.

So all in all, really not something special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But SCP-2602 doesn't make you think you are a library, does it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good points, also changed to reflect this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: remember, 'are or have been", and not "non-fish exposure" but just "electrocution and/or water damage".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This rephrasing works for me. I will include it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can get behind that phrasing. Quite thematically appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personnel who are or who have previously been toasters or libraries are not to be assigned to SCP-1839, due to the risk of electrocution and/or water damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Wikiwalk reasoning
Hide Wikiwalk reasoning

Hey folks, so the Wikiwalk team is currently looking at adding two crosslinks to this article, specifically to SCP-426 and SCP-2602. The reasoning behind these crosslinks is to incorporate a connection to articles with similar effects, specifically making the reader believe that either themselves or an object is something, regardless of what they or it might actually be. The planned crosslink would be added into the containment procedures, delineating that researchers previously assigned to either 426 or 2602 should not be subsequently transferred to 1839.

Editing this:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-1839 is to be kept in containment locker 534 at Site-19. Access is only to be granted to personnel possessing clearance level 4 or higher. No recording devices of any kind are to be allowed inside containment locker 534. Personnel are to note that they are a fish.

To this:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-1839 is to be kept in containment locker 534 at Site-19. Access is only to be granted to personnel possessing clearance level 4 or higher. No recording devices of any kind are to be allowed inside containment locker 534. Personnel are to note that they are a fish. Personnel who are or have previously been toasters or libraries should not be assigned to SCP-1839, due to the risk of electrocution or water damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added accordingly. Interestingly, the anomaly is memetic, and infohazard, AND meta. While containing infectious information as a book, referring to the book itself poses an infohazard (as shown in the meta document-affected article).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't this an infohazard? or am i interpreting this wrong. If so, shouldn't this be tagged as one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just got in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting this article is exactly the kind of mistake a fish would make, and has since been remedied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The real question is, though, do you like fish sticks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. U. R. A. Fish

nice.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha, This Article's really funny! I want to cackle in laughter but i'am a fish, therefore i cannot.

but i doubt it being from the mainlist, yeah let's say the writing and everything's good, yada yada but the gimmicks and silliness is too much. i could upvote it if it is a -J Article tho.

These Fish Puns are really Kraken me up.

But Nevertheless a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why the restriction to level 4 (and logically only C4, which seems to be like Site Directors and MTF commanders and whatnot)? Why do you need broad need-to-know access to almost everything the foundation does just to do research on this one scip and think you are a fish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this… name seems familiar…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to reread that first description paragraph multiple times. When I finally moved on, a smile crept across my face as I read further. Nicely done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Subjects will hold their breath until expiration."

shouldn't that be, until they lose consciousness?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am the sharks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New favorite article. *bloop* Anybody else get the feeling it's a bit dry in here? *bloop bloop*
I gave the +100, which is odd, considering the fact that I am a fish and shouldn't be able to click with my fins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't this be tagged as meta?

I'd change it myself, but I'm not so good at typing anymore, due to the fact that I might be a fish…

Edit: Due to my fish-vision, I could not see that it already was noted as Meta. My fishpologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the writer of SCP-426, I want to poohpooh all the people who are calling SCP-1839 out on being too similar. It does share a lot of elements but is distinct enough to stand on its own. And, yes, I do realize that I'm a bit late to the party on this.

Though I do wonder why this reminds me of my last Astronavigation exam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted this, it was hard to do do with fins, but I managed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit, I got curious as to what fish lips on a human looked like, and I found myself doing it in front of a mirror to check. You got me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really get how you even managed to write this article. Computers don't work underwater, and I don't think you could survive in open air for very long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I laughed a little too hard at this. It blends funny with creep in good measure, as how I imagine the fishes in a schizo's head sound. I like this fish because stands out on its borderline -J fish, which is rare with most fish on the fish, or other fish, in fish. Other fish don't always fish the fish or fish the fish, but you do, and I fish you the best.

Chips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know that I'm a fish, but is my mother a fish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Salmon Corbette, you are a fish brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I was amused.
I would have laughed but I am a fish

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all honesty, my favorite SCP's are the ones that could easily be seen as comedic. Anomalies would obviously vary from terrifying to silly and the silly ones are still terrifying to fathom becoming a victim of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beat me to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could a subject maintain two, mutually exclusive delusions? Would one dominate the other? Would he hybridize them, "I'm a toaster-fish, I make golden deliciousness under the sea"? Would they cancel each other out, like Mr. Burns' immune system? Imma try and write a tale where this happens.

Please write this tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It reads like I imagine a schizophrenic's delusional voices sound. Constantly slipping in little snippets and asides, an endless stream of little nudges until one becomes convinced of some alternative reality.

That hyper-link is a glaring disruption and a big let-down. It jumps out and grabs the eye, interupting the flow of reading. It'd be fine if there was something underneath, but if there's no page, why make it a big, practically glowing eye-sore?

After reading the comments, I'm left wondering what would happen if you memetically "crossed the streams". What would happen if a subject was simultaneously exposed to this and 426?

Could a subject maintain two, mutually exclusive delusions? Would one dominate the other? Would he hybridize them, "I'm a toaster-fish, I make golden deliciousness under the sea"? Would they cancel each other out, like Mr. Burns' immune system? Imma try and write a tale where this happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YES, seriously, YES!

I love SCPs that affect even their report, those are one of the best, if not the bestestly bestest best.

If I had a fish, I'd show it this article so so much!

People who downvote this make me a saaaaad panda fish. But what can you expect from fishes. I want more of this. I think I flag this SCP as the positive example of what to make up and write. So I found both my positive and negative examples today. Not bad from a fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sleeve of the book shows a rough, colourful diagram of the reproductive system of an Atlantic blue marlin, similar to what the reader currently is.

And this is where I upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are a fish. Therefore, you must eat yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation was that knowing about it did cause minor psychological effects and the fish references in the text were supposed to be in the mind of the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only way this could be better is if the author's name was Hugh Arafess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My preferred system of tagging is just shoving as many as possible in and letting Mackenzie get all angry and remove the unnecessary ones.

To be honest, I'd rather the meta bit be left up to interpretation. As the tag master, I'll let you decide whether or not ambiguity is a qualifier for a tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if bees were fish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I about choked when I saw every mind-affecting tag on this SCP. I removed infohazard because I'm fairly sure that simply knowing that the SCP exists doesn't infect you with it, but Salman needs to decide, as the author, whether this qualifies for meta. meta means that it affects its documentation; this means that the SCP itself is causing the documentation to become this way and it isn't just an infected researcher having access to the records.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this was a -J then I would upvote it, but I'm neutral on it as a mainlist item. I find it a little bit too silly, and I find that gimmicks in the article often ruin suspension of disbelief for me, I always feel that the article should be as they are written, not as they might be read by someone suffering from effects of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am bees. Inside a fish. Burning on a toaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, they're funny, and I know it's the main hook, but it's still just a little off-putting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still seems a bit random. :/

I mean, they're funny, and I know it's the main hook, but it's still just a little off-putting. Guess I'm just not in the right mindset for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're making the assumption that the article author also wrote in the bits about you being a fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of myself. I tried to link you to me, but then I was bees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm probably just dumb as fuck, but what does the book making you think you're a fish have to do with people writing about it insisting that you're one as well? Is it explicitly stated anywhere that the latter is an effect as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a little of bees. I like bees and I like this, for a moment I even felt compelled to make a fish mouth but it is late and I am tired. Bees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed, but it's not a -J. I honestly think it's too silly, and we've already got the toaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was pretty funny, but (as Hyaenaboy and others have pointed out) the repetition of "you are a fish" comes off as a lazy choice of words. I like the idea that the memetic effect gets stronger / less subtle as you read through the article, but it wasn't clear that that was the reason behind your phrasing. Between that, the similarity to 426, and my complete inability to take this article seriously, I can't upvote this in good conscience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I may be missing something here. Isn't this basically just SCP-426 with toaster replaced with fish, and it's infectious via media now? It's well-written, but the knock-off value made me flinch too hard to click the +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yeah I totally understand its valid. But it doesn't mean it doesn't piss me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone left the air confishoning on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never heard of a catfish?
